flashcards.front: What is decomposition in relational database design?
flashcards.back: Decomposition is the process of breaking a relation into smaller, well-structured relations to eliminate redundancy and prevent anomalies (update, insertion, deletion).
A decomposition is lossless (lossless-join) if the natural join of the decomposed relations yields exactly the original relation without generating spurious tuples.

flashcards.front: What is the lossless-join test for decomposition?
flashcards.back: A decomposition of R into R1, R2 is lossless if (R1 ∩ R2) → R1 or (R1 ∩ R2) → R2 holds in the functional dependencies. The common attributes must functionally determine either relation.

A decomposition is dependency-preserving if the closure of the union of projected FDs on each decomposed relation equals the closure of the original FDs. All original dependencies can be enforced without joins.
A relation R is in BCNF if for every non-trivial FD X → Y, X is a superkey of R. All determinants must be candidate keys.
A relation R is in 3NF if for every FD X → A (where A is non-prime), either X is a superkey or A is part of some candidate key. Solves redundancy while preserving dependencies.
Update anomalies occur when changing data in one row fails to update all related rows, leading to inconsistent data. Caused by redundancy.
Insertion anomalies occur when certain data cannot be inserted without adding other unrelated data (e.g., needing to know a student's course before enrolling them).
Deletion anomalies occur when deleting one piece of data unintentionally removes other related information (e.g., deleting last student from a course deletes course info).

flashcards.front: What is a trivial functional dependency?
flashcards.back: An FD X → Y is trivial if Y is a subset of X (i.e., X → X). These are always satisfied and don't provide useful constraints.
A superkey is a set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples in a relation. It functionally determines all attributes of the relation.
A candidate key is a minimal superkey - a minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples. No proper subset can be a superkey.

flashcards.front: Define canonical cover of functional dependencies
flashcards.back: A canonical cover is a minimal set of FDs that is equivalent to the original set. It has no extraneous attributes and no redundant dependencies.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between BCNF and 3NF decomposition?
flashcards.back: BCNF decomposition may not always preserve dependencies, while 3NF always allows dependency preservation. 3NF is less strict but more practical.

flashcards.front: What is a spurious tuple in decomposition?
flashcards.back: A spurious tuple is an extra tuple that appears in the join result but was not in the original relation, indicating lossless-join property is violated.
